Breast Cancer.description: Lifetime risk of developing breast cancer. Risk increases significantly with BRCA1/2 mutations, family history, and hormonal factors.
Breast Cancer.age_factor: Risk increases with age; median diagnosis age is 62. Annual mammography recommended starting at 40-50.
Breast Cancer.sex_factor: Female: ~12.5% lifetime risk. Male: ~0.1% lifetime risk.

Ovarian Cancer.description: Lifetime risk of ovarian cancer. Often diagnosed at advanced stage due to lack of effective screening.
Ovarian Cancer.age_factor: Peak incidence ages 55-64. Risk increases with age and nulliparity.
Ovarian Cancer.sex_factor: Female only. BRCA1 carriers: 40-60% lifetime risk. BRCA2 carriers: 15-30% lifetime risk.

Prostate Cancer.description: Lifetime risk of prostate cancer in men. One of the most common cancers worldwide.
Prostate Cancer.age_factor: Rare before age 40. Risk increases sharply after 50. Median age at diagnosis: 66.
Prostate Cancer.sex_factor: Male only. African American men: ~1.7x higher incidence than Caucasian men.

Colorectal Cancer.description: Lifetime risk of colorectal cancer. Screening significantly reduces mortality through early detection and polyp removal.
Colorectal Cancer.age_factor: Risk increases after age 50. Screening recommended starting at 45. Rising incidence in younger adults.
Colorectal Cancer.sex_factor: Males: ~4.5% lifetime risk. Females: ~4.1% lifetime risk.

Lung Cancer.description: Lifetime risk of lung cancer. Leading cause of cancer death worldwide. Primarily driven by smoking.
Lung Cancer.age_factor: Most cases diagnosed after age 65. Average age at diagnosis: 70.
Lung Cancer.sex_factor: Males: ~6.7% lifetime risk. Females: ~6.1% lifetime risk. Non-smokers: ~0.2-0.4% lifetime risk.

Coronary Artery Disease.description: Prevalence of coronary artery disease in adults. Leading cause of death globally.
Coronary Artery Disease.age_factor: Risk doubles each decade after age 45. Majority of events occur after age 65.
Coronary Artery Disease.sex_factor: Males: ~7.4% prevalence. Females: ~5.3% prevalence. Female risk increases sharply post-menopause.

Type 2 Diabetes.description: Prevalence of type 2 diabetes in adults. Prevalence is rapidly increasing worldwide due to obesity and sedentary lifestyles.
Type 2 Diabetes.age_factor: Prevalence increases with age: 4.2% ages 18-44, 17.5% ages 45-64, 26.8% ages 65+.
Type 2 Diabetes.sex_factor: Males: ~11.3%. Females: ~9.0%. Higher prevalence in certain ethnic groups.

Alzheimer's Disease.description: Lifetime risk of Alzheimer's disease after age 45. Most common cause of dementia, affecting memory, thinking, and behavior.
Alzheimer's Disease.age_factor: Prevalence doubles every 5 years after age 65. ~5% at 65, ~30% at 85. Average age of onset: 75.
Alzheimer's Disease.sex_factor: Females: ~12% lifetime risk. Males: ~9% lifetime risk. Women's higher risk partly due to longer lifespan.

Parkinson's Disease.description: Lifetime risk of Parkinson's disease. Second most common neurodegenerative disorder after Alzheimer's.
Parkinson's Disease.age_factor: Mean age of onset: 60. Prevalence: ~1% over age 60, ~4% over age 80. Early-onset (<50) accounts for ~10% of cases.
Parkinson's Disease.sex_factor: Males: ~2% lifetime risk. Females: ~1.3% lifetime risk. Male:female ratio approximately 1.5:1.

Atrial Fibrillation.description: Prevalence of atrial fibrillation in adults. Most common sustained cardiac arrhythmia. Major risk factor for stroke.
Atrial Fibrillation.age_factor: Prevalence increases with age: 0.1% under 55, 3.8% ages 60-69, 9% ages 80+. Lifetime risk ~25%.

Celiac Disease.description: Prevalence of celiac disease. Autoimmune disorder triggered by dietary gluten in genetically susceptible individuals (HLA-DQ2/DQ8 positive).
Celiac Disease.age_factor: Can present at any age. Bimodal distribution: childhood (8-12 months after gluten introduction) and ages 30-50.
Celiac Disease.sex_factor: Females: ~1.5x more commonly diagnosed than males. True prevalence may be similar.

Hereditary Hemochromatosis.description: Prevalence of hereditary hemochromatosis (clinical disease). HFE C282Y homozygosity affects ~1 in 200 Northern Europeans, but clinical penetrance is ~28% in males, ~1% in females.
Hereditary Hemochromatosis.age_factor: Clinical presentation typically ages 40-60 in males, post-menopausal in females (menstruation is protective).
Hereditary Hemochromatosis.sex_factor: Males: ~28% penetrance for C282Y homozygotes. Females: ~1% penetrance due to menstrual iron loss.

Venous Thromboembolism.description: Annual incidence of VTE (DVT and PE). Third most common cardiovascular disease. Factor V Leiden and prothrombin G20210A are major genetic risk factors.
Venous Thromboembolism.age_factor: Incidence increases exponentially with age: ~0.01% under 40, ~0.1% ages 40-59, ~1% over 80.
Venous Thromboembolism.sex_factor: Slightly higher in males overall. Higher in females during reproductive years (pregnancy, oral contraceptives).

Age-Related Macular Degeneration.description: Prevalence of AMD in adults over 40. Leading cause of vision loss in people over 50 in developed countries.
Age-Related Macular Degeneration.sex_factor: Females: slightly higher prevalence, possibly due to longer lifespan.

Inflammatory Bowel Disease (Crohn's).description: Prevalence of Crohn's disease. Chronic inflammatory condition of the GI tract with genetic and environmental components.
Inflammatory Bowel Disease (Crohn's).age_factor: Bimodal age distribution: peak onset 15-30 years, second peak 50-70 years.
Inflammatory Bowel Disease (Crohn's).sex_factor: Slightly more common in females. Geographic variation: highest in Northern Europe and North America.

Inflammatory Bowel Disease (Ulcerative C.description: Prevalence of ulcerative colitis. Chronic inflammation of the colon and rectum.
Inflammatory Bowel Disease (Ulcerative C.age_factor: Peak onset ages 15-30 with second smaller peak ages 50-70.

Multiple Sclerosis.description: Prevalence of multiple sclerosis. Autoimmune disease affecting the central nervous system myelin sheath.
Multiple Sclerosis.age_factor: Typically diagnosed between ages 20-40. Rarely presents before 10 or after 60.
Multiple Sclerosis.sex_factor: Females: 2-3x more common than males. Female:male ratio has been increasing over time.

Rheumatoid Arthritis.description: Prevalence of rheumatoid arthritis. Chronic autoimmune inflammatory disorder affecting joints.
Rheumatoid Arthritis.age_factor: Peak onset ages 30-60. Prevalence increases with age, reaching ~2% in those over 60.
Rheumatoid Arthritis.sex_factor: Females: 2-3x more common than males. Gender gap narrows with advancing age.

Cystic Fibrosis.description: Birth prevalence of cystic fibrosis. Most common life-limiting autosomal recessive condition in Caucasians. Carrier frequency ~1 in 25.
Cystic Fibrosis.age_factor: Diagnosed in infancy/childhood via newborn screening. Median survival now >50 years with modern therapies.
Cystic Fibrosis.sex_factor: Males and females equally affected. Males typically infertile. Females may have reduced fertility.

Sickle Cell Disease.description: Birth prevalence in the US. Much higher in African Americans (~1 in 365). Sickle cell trait (carrier) affects ~8% of African Americans.
Sickle Cell Disease.age_factor: Diagnosed at birth via newborn screening. Symptoms begin at 5-6 months when fetal hemoglobin declines.
Sickle Cell Disease.sex_factor: Males and females equally affected (autosomal recessive).

Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Deficiency.description: Prevalence of severe AAT deficiency (Pi*ZZ). Significantly underdiagnosed; estimated that only 10% of affected individuals are diagnosed.
Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Deficiency.age_factor: Lung disease typically presents ages 30-50, especially in smokers. Liver disease can present at any age.
Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Deficiency.sex_factor: Males and females equally affected genetically. Males may develop lung disease earlier due to higher smoking rates.

G6PD Deficiency.description: Global prevalence of G6PD deficiency. Most common enzyme deficiency worldwide, affecting ~400 million people. Prevalence varies greatly by geography.
G6PD Deficiency.age_factor: Present from birth. Hemolytic crises can occur at any age upon exposure to triggers.
G6PD Deficiency.sex_factor: X-linked: primarily affects males. Carrier females may have variable expression due to X-inactivation.

Familial Hypercholesterolemia.description: Prevalence of heterozygous familial hypercholesterolemia (1 in 250). Homozygous FH is rare (~1 in 300,000). Most individuals are undiagnosed.
Familial Hypercholesterolemia.age_factor: Elevated LDL present from birth. Untreated men: MI risk by age 50 is ~50%. Untreated women: MI risk by age 60 is ~30%.
Familial Hypercholesterolemia.sex_factor: Males: earlier cardiovascular events. Females: protection before menopause.

Hereditary Thrombophilia.description: Combined prevalence of Factor V Leiden and Prothrombin G20210A heterozygosity in Caucasian populations.
Hereditary Thrombophilia.age_factor: First VTE event typically ages 20-50. Risk increases with age, surgery, immobility, and pregnancy.
Hereditary Thrombophilia.sex_factor: Females at higher risk during reproductive years due to pregnancy and oral contraceptive use.

Melanoma.description: Lifetime risk of melanoma. Incidence has been rising steadily. UV exposure is the primary modifiable risk factor.
Melanoma.age_factor: Can occur at any age. Median age at diagnosis: 65. One of the most common cancers in young adults.
Melanoma.sex_factor: Males: higher lifetime risk (~2.8%). Females: lower lifetime risk (~1.8%). Males more likely to have fatal melanoma.

Type 1 Diabetes.description: Prevalence of type 1 diabetes. Autoimmune destruction of pancreatic beta cells. Strong HLA association.
Type 1 Diabetes.age_factor: Peak incidence ages 4-7 and 10-14. Increasingly diagnosed in adults. Can occur at any age.
Type 1 Diabetes.sex_factor: Slightly more common in males in populations of European ancestry.

Obesity.description: Prevalence of obesity (BMI ≥30) in US adults. Major risk factor for cardiovascular disease, diabetes, and cancer.
Obesity.sex_factor: Females: ~41.9%. Males: ~43.0%. Severe obesity (BMI ≥40) more common in females.

Osteoporosis.description: Prevalence of osteoporosis in adults over 50. Major cause of fractures in elderly.
Osteoporosis.age_factor: Prevalence increases with age: ~10% ages 50-59, ~18% ages 60-69, ~35% ages 70+.
Osteoporosis.sex_factor: Females: ~25% over age 50. Males: ~5% over age 50. Post-menopausal bone loss is a major factor.
